sources_exist.note: All seven canonical URL occurrences resolve to accessible pages. The repeated HVAC valuation URL was audited independently for buy_mult and exit_mult as required.

figures_match_sources.note: The current-adoption and HARDI demographic figures match, and the valuation page supports broad buy/exit ranges, but inputs.historical_analogs.url does not show the quoted 54%-to-72% series or an eight-year HVAC/plumbing path to 50%; inputs.pricing_structure.url does not show the claimed 90%, 39%, or $199-$399 figures; and inputs.buy_mult.url supplies ranges rather than evidence that 5.0x is the median for the specified target band. These unsupported inputs affect A, C, and M. The BORDERLINE flag therefore increases rather than cures the need for supported inputs.

judgment_inputs_plausible.note: Most role fractions, pi_moat=0.60, and the physical-trade DURABLE framing are plausible and bounded. However, t50_years=8 is derived from the unsupported historical analog, while pi_dist=0.55 relies materially on an unsupported claimed revenue/pricing mix; the judgments cannot be accepted against the cited evidence.

rubric_consistent.note: The record applies the frozen formulas and assigns PE/M&A evidence only to B and M. The deterministic dataset objects match the provided dataset exactly. BORDERLINE: |S - pass cut 3.00| = 0.1440 is explicitly reviewed: proximity to the pass cut provides no acceptance exception. ARITH_DELTA_WITHIN_TOL: nonzero stored-vs-recomputed deltas on B, S is explicitly reviewed: the flagged differences are within the orchestrator tolerance and are not treated as a rubric defect.

cross_checks_honest.note: All four mandatory cross-checks are present, distinguish operator cost reduction from product replacement, and candidly describe pricing deflation and the non-PE basis for V and A. Their quantitative support remains deficient through the failed historical-analog and pricing citations, but the cross-check narratives themselves are internally candid.

source_audits.note: The accessible 2026 tracker reports 27 active US HVAC PE roll-up platforms in its quick answer, 18 under its narrower publicly verified HVAC-deal criterion, and approximately 60 Apex add-ons in 2025. This supports at least 25 active consolidators for the HVAC-dominant subsegment, although the record's wording about 23+ plumbing platforms is not what this cited page says.

The page supports PE add-on ranges of 4.0-8.0x EBITDA and reports typical $2M EBITDA HVAC pricing around 6.0-7.0x, with GF Data's broader NAICS 238 $10M-$25M TEV cohort at 5.7x. It does not establish that 5.0x is the median actually paid for the record's $1M-$5M EBITDA band, and the record's stated SDE-to-EBITDA conversion is not evidenced by this page.

ServiceTitan states that 1,032 commercial and residential contractors across seven trades were surveyed, 12% had embedded AI, and 34% were experimenting. The 12% adoption input is directly supported, though the accessible page does not show the record's additional assertion that 57% of respondents were HVAC/plumbing/electrical.

The page reports a 9.0-13.0x range and typical 10.0-12.0x for $10M-$25M EBITDA regional platforms, PKF's north-of-10x statement, First Page Sage's 9.3-10.8x residential $5M-$10M range, and the J&S Mechanical 8.0-10.0x transaction. These benchmarks support 10.5x as a reasonable exit input for the stated tier.

The accessible article reports general FSM adoption statistics, including 67% fully integrated mobile solutions and a stated 41% increase from 2020, but it does not contain the quoted rise from 54% in 2020 to 72% in 2024 or evidence that HVAC/plumbing CRM/FSM moved from sub-10% to about 50% over eight years.

HARDI reports nearly 1,100 contractor responses, 30% age 55-64, 23% over 65, and 61% owners or executives. Treating half of the 55-64 group as 60-64 yields the record's disclosed estimate of 38% age 60+, although the page does not independently document the separate 35% succession-plan assertion.

The page recommends flat-rate pricing for common repairs and installs and time-and-materials for diagnostics and complex jobs, describing most firms as hybrid. It does not report that 90% of residential work is flat-rate, that preventive maintenance is 39% of US HVAC-services revenue, or that maintenance agreements cost $199-$399 per year.

reasons: inputs.historical_analogs.url (https://fieldservicesoftware.io/blog/field-service-management-software-statistics/) does not support the quoted 54%-to-72% mobile-work-order series or an approximately eight-year HVAC/plumbing transition from sub-10% to 50%. Re-run with a source that directly reports a comparable industry's dated adoption series and scope, then derive t50_years from that evidence.

inputs.pricing_structure.url (https://www.housecallpro.com/resources/how-to-price-hvac-services/) supports only a qualitative hybrid of flat-rate and time-and-materials pricing, not the quoted 90% flat-rate share, 39% maintenance-revenue share, or $199-$399 agreement price. Re-run with direct, scoped evidence for the billing/revenue mix used in pi_dist and the pricing-deflation cross-check.

inputs.buy_mult.url (https://ctacquisitions.com/guides/hvac-business-valuation/) gives broad ranges and indicates typical $2M EBITDA pricing around 6.0-7.0x, but does not establish 5.0x as the median actually paid for the specified $1M-$5M EBITDA targets or substantiate the stated SDE conversion. Re-run with transaction/cohort evidence matching target size and EBITDA normalization, or transparently mark a supported estimate without calling it an observed median.
flags_reviewed: ARITH_DELTA_WITHIN_TOL: nonzero stored-vs-recomputed deltas on B, S
